title: The Precision-Recall Curve
concept: The Precision-Recall Curve plots precision against recall at different confidence thresholds, visualizing the model's trade-off.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main drawback of having high recall but low precision?

options.text: The model outputs too many false alarms (false positives)
The model misses the main objects
The model only runs on CPUs

exercises.explain: High recall with low precision means the model catches objects but predicts many false alarms.

cards.title: The Sensitivity Knob
cards.body: If you tune a metal detector, you can turn up the sensitivity to find every coin (high recall), but it will beep at bottle caps and nails (low precision). Turning down the sensitivity only beeps at pure gold (high precision) but misses coins. Plotting these scores at different settings creates a curve.

cards.title: Precision-Recall Curve
cards.body: A Precision-Recall Curve is a graph that plots precision on the y-axis and recall on the x-axis. It is generated by evaluating the model at different confidence thresholds (from 1.0 down to 0.0) to show the performance trade-off.

cards.title: The Volume Slider
cards.body: Think of adjusting the volume slider on a microphone. High volume catches every whisper (high recall) but introduces background hiss (low precision). Plotting the recording quality at every volume level creates the curve.

cards.title: Generating the Curve
cards.body: At confidence threshold 0.9, the model only keeps highly confident boxes: precision is 95%, recall is 20%. At threshold 0.1, it keeps almost all boxes: precision drops to 15%, but recall climbs to 98%. These coordinate points form the curve.

cards.title: Not a simple diagonal line
cards.body: It's a myth that the curve is always smooth or straight. Because predictions are discrete, the curve can wiggle up and down locally as you adjust thresholds, though it generally slopes downwards as recall increases.

cards.title: Plotting the Curve
cards.body: Visualizing the typical shape of a Precision-Recall graph:

left.text:
High confidence threshold:
High precision, low recall.

right.text:
Low confidence threshold:
Low precision, high recall.

exercises.prompt: What variables are plotted on the axes of a Precision-Recall Curve?
exercises.explain: The Precision-Recall Curve plots precision (Y) against recall (X) across varying thresholds.

options.text: Precision on the Y-axis and Recall on the X-axis
Loss on the Y-axis and Epoch on the X-axis
IoU on the Y-axis and Confidence on the X-axis
True Positives on the Y-axis and False Positives on the X-axis

exercises.prompt: True or False: As we lower the confidence threshold of a detector, recall generally increases while precision generally decreases.
exercises.explain: True. Lowering the threshold allows more boxes to pass, finding more real objects (raising recall) but introducing more false alarms (lowering precision).
Varying the confidence score threshold (e.g. from 0.99 down to 0.01) creates the data points for the curve.
exercises.sentence: Points on the Precision-Recall curve are generated by varying the model's ___ threshold.

exercises.prompt: Which curve trend represents a model's typical behavior?
exercises.explain: As recall increases (by letting more predictions pass), precision generally drops due to false alarms.

options.text: Precision starts high and declines as recall increases
Precision starts at 0 and rises linearly to 100
The curve remains a flat constant horizontal line at 0.5

exercises.prompt: You are comparing two models. Model A's curve stays close to the top-right corner of the plot (high precision and high recall). Model B's curve drops quickly toward the bottom-left. Which model is better, and why?

options.text: Model A, because it maintains high precision even as recall increases, meaning it makes very few mistakes while finding most objects.
Model B, because it reaches low precision faster.
Model B, because it runs faster on CPU.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The closer the curve is to the top-right corner (representing 100% precision and 100% recall), the better the model's performance. Next: let's learn how we summarize this curve into a single number!
